claim: A 2021 Lancet Planetary Health study by Burke et al. estimated that long-term wildfire-smoke PM2.5 exposure is associated with 339,000 premature deaths annually worldwide, with smoke contributing up to 25 percent of ambient PM2.5 in some fire-prone regions and potentially 2-3 times more toxic per unit mass than PM2.5 from other sources.

source.title: Global estimates of mortality associated with long-term exposure to wildfire-related PM2.5

method: Reviewed smoke-exposure estimation methodology using satellite AOD, health-impact modeling with concentration-response functions, and differential toxicity analysis comparing smoke vs. non-smoke PM2.5.
limitations: Smoke-exposure estimates rely on satellite AOD retrievals from MODIS, which have cloud-cover gaps and reduced reliability over bright surfaces and high-altitude terrain.

The differential toxicity finding (2-3x) is based on oxidative-potential measurements from a limited number of fire types and regions — it is not yet a consensus finding and requires replication across boreal, temperate, and tropical fire regimes.

Concentration-response functions are derived primarily from North American and Australian studies; transferability to LMIC populations with different baseline health status and exposure patterns is uncertain.
Mortality estimates are modeled, not observed — they depend on population exposure, baseline mortality rates, and concentration-response assumptions that each carry uncertainty.

claim: NOAA Hazard Mapping System provides daily satellite-derived smoke-plume polygons at approximately 1 km resolution from 2006 to present, classifying smoke density into light, medium, and heavy categories from GOES and polar-orbiting satellite imagery.

source.title: NOAA Hazard Mapping System Smoke Product

method: Reviewed HMS product documentation for detection algorithm, spatial resolution, temporal coverage, smoke-density classification, and known limitations. Confirmed archive availability from 2006.
limitations: HMS smoke-plume classification is based on visible-wavelength satellite imagery and cannot detect smoke under cloud cover or at night.

Smoke-density categories (light, medium, heavy) are analyst-assigned, not algorithmically calibrated to PM2.5 concentration — they indicate smoke presence and relative thickness, not air-quality impact.
Plume polygons may not capture thin smoke layers or ground-hugging smoke inversions that are common during stable atmospheric conditions.
The product was designed for aviation weather support, not air-quality epidemiology — using it as a PM2.5 exposure proxy requires ground-monitor validation.

claim: GEOS-Chem global chemical-transport model provides composition-resolved PM2.5 estimates including smoke-specific black carbon and organic carbon fractions at approximately 0.25-degree resolution, enabling smoke-specific exposure estimation when combined with fire-emissions inventories.
method: Reviewed GEOS-Chem model documentation for PM2.5 species resolution, fire-emissions integration (GFED, GFAS), spatial resolution, and validation studies.
limitations: GEOS-Chem PM2.5 estimates depend on fire-emissions inventories (GFED, GFAS) that have their own burned-area and emission-factor uncertainties.
Model resolution (0.25 deg, approximately 25 km) is too coarse to capture near-source smoke plume variability — it smooths local smoke exposure gradients.

Model performance varies by region — validation studies show better performance in North America than in Southeast Asia or Sub-Saharan Africa where emission inventories and meteorological inputs are less constrained.
Smoke-specific PM2.5 fractions in the model are sensitive to assumed organic-matter-to-organic-carbon ratios that vary by fuel type and combustion conditions.
